explanation: Legacy term: a Winchester disk is the sealed fixed hard-disk assembly with several rigid platters mounted on one spindle, i.e. a disk stack. The distractors describe the opposite properties: it is not removable (the platters are sealed in with the heads) and not flexible (that is a floppy).

CD-RW expands to CD-ReWritable, the optical format whose recording layer can be erased and rewritten, so it is the reusable one. CD-R/CD-ROM are write-once or read-only, DVD names a capacity family rather than a reusability class, and RPM is a rotation speed unit, not a medium.

Answer unverified: the source key marks D (Store) but the review panel derived B (Input), and the two disagree. The stem is a garbled version of the standard input-process-output-storage item: 'gathering' data is the intake step (input), while storing is a separate function listed elsewhere in the same series. Treat this item as ambiguous and learn the four functions rather than the key.

Answer unverified: the item has no key and none of the printed options is correct as written. Copying software from distribution media onto the hard disk is installation. Download and Upload are network transfers, Configuration is the step after install, and Storage names no process, so option E ('Escalation') is almost certainly an OCR corruption of 'Installation'.

An open document is held in RAM, the volatile working memory the CPU reads and writes while you edit; it only becomes permanent when you save it to disk. ROM is read-only and cannot hold your edits, and the CPU itself only holds a few words in registers, not a document.

In the memory hierarchy, cache is the level that is fastest, most expensive per byte, smallest, and managed by hardware rather than by the OS. Main memory and disk are larger, slower, cheaper per byte, and their allocation is managed by the operating system, which is the discriminator the stem is testing with 'managed by computer system hardware'.

Babbage's Analytical Engine held its numbers in the 'store', built from columns of geared counter wheels, with each wheel position representing one decimal digit. Cards are the classic trap: punched cards fed the Engine its program and input, but they were not the memory unit.

The motherboard is the main printed circuit board that physically carries the CPU socket, RAM slots, ROM/BIOS chip, and the expansion slots for add-in cards. The bus is only the set of wires that carries signals between those components, not the board that holds them.

Cache memory sits between the CPU and main memory, holding recently and frequently used data in faster SRAM so the CPU stalls less often on slow RAM accesses. Virtual memory is the trap: it works in the opposite direction, extending RAM onto the much slower disk, so it adds latency rather than removing it.

In paging, the logical (virtual) address space is divided into equal-size blocks called pages, and physical memory is divided into same-size blocks called frames. Frames is the deliberate trap: the two terms are the same size and are used as a pair, so remember logical to pages, physical to frames.

RAM is by definition random-access memory: any location can be read or written in the same time regardless of address. ROM and PROM are also randomly addressable but are read-only, which is why 'All of these' is the intended trap and RAM is the keyed answer.

Secondary storage (hard disk, SSD, optical, tape) is non-volatile, so its contents survive a power-off. RAM, which is primary storage, is volatile and loses its contents when power is removed, which is why both A and D are wrong.

Floppy disc, magnetic tape, hard disc, and optical disc are all non-volatile media that retain data without power, so every listed option qualifies as 'permanent' memory and 'All of these' is correct. The trap is picking a single familiar medium such as hard disc when the category is what is being tested.

Main memory works directly with the CPU: the processor fetches instructions and data from it over the system bus on every cycle. RAM is the trap here, because main memory IS RAM, so choosing A makes the statement circular rather than identifying the component it partners with.

SRAM stands for Static Random-Access Memory. 'Static' means each bit is held in a flip-flop and needs no periodic refresh, unlike DRAM whose capacitors must be recharged, which is why SRAM is faster and is used for cache.

Answer unverified: this item is broken and has no key. There is no device class called a 'super computer floppy disc', and no floppy-family format has any of the offered capacities (3.5in HD is 1.44 MB, ED is 2.88 MB, LS-120 SuperDisk is 120 MB, Zip is 100/250/750 MB). The 400 to 700 M ladder in the options is CD-ROM capacity, not floppy. Only 'None of these' is defensible; do not memorise a number here.

ROM is the built-in, non-volatile memory written at manufacture that holds the firmware/BIOS the machine needs to start up. RAM is wrong because it is volatile and empty at power-on, and PROM/EPROM are user-programmable variants rather than the memory that ships built into the machine.

Virtual memory reserves an area of the hard disk (the page or swap file) and lets the OS move pages between it and RAM, so programs can address more memory than is physically installed. Option B is the trap: the whole point of virtual memory is that the backing store is on disk, not in RAM.

USB stands for Universal Serial Bus. It is 'serial' because data is sent one bit at a time over a differential pair, and 'universal' because a single connector standard replaced the older keyboard, mouse, serial, and parallel ports.

Off-syllabus psychology trivia: a memory experiment has a presentation (study) phase where stimuli are shown and a test phase where retrieval is measured, so recall is elicited in the test phase. 'Material' and 'distinction' are not phase names at all.
